original_abstract:
As large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) continue to grow in size, the traditional paradigm of full-parameter fine-tuning has become increasingly impractical due to the massive computational and storage costs involved. This has spurred the development of parameter-efficient adaptation techniques that optimize only a small subset of model weights while keeping the majority frozen. In this work, we propose the unified term **"delta-tuning"** to describe this field, where "delta" represents the small parameter changes made to the system during adaptation. 

We provide a formal problem definition and a comprehensive categorization of existing methods to better understand their underlying mechanics and structural differences. Beyond empirical observations, we analyze the theoretical foundations of delta-tuning through the lenses of optimization and optimal control, offering an interpretation of why stimulating a few parameters can effectively steer massive models. Our research includes an extensive empirical evaluation across more than 100 natural language processing tasks, investigating performance, convergence, and the "power of scale." Our findings demonstrate that delta-tuning is not only a practical necessity for utilizing large-scale PLMs but is also supported by robust theoretical properties that allow it to match or even surpass traditional fine-tuning in efficiency and effectiveness.
humanized_abstract:
The recent large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) are constantly increasing in size. While they demonstrate impressive performance, the full-parameter fine-tuning of such large models is not efficient, because it demands tremendous amount of computation resources and storage space. To alleviate the challenges, parameter-efficient methods for adapting large models have drawn significant attention in the community. These methods update only a small fraction of model parameters and leave the overwhelming majority of parameters unchanged. For this family of updates, we coin the term “delta-tuning”. The term “delta” symbolizes the small amounts of changes that are introduced to the system during the adaptation process.

We provide a detailed formal problem statement together with a taxonomy of various techniques and try to illuminate the underlying principles and structural distinctions of these approaches. We then relate delta learning to the broad area of optimization and optimal control, which enables us to shine a light on the mysteries of the so-called couple of parameters that need to be adjusted in order to control the behavior of enormous models. We carry out a thorough experimental evaluation on more than one hundred benchmarks in natural language processing that aims to assess the achievable gains in performance, training speed and the so called power of scale. In conclusion, our experiments (coupled with our theoretical analysis) confirm that the need for delta learning as a tool for exploiting huge pre-trained language models (PLMs) is very real and also that it is a phenomenon that stands on solid optimization grounds, since delta learning can routinely match, and often beat, fine-tuning in terms of training speed and also in terms of its final performance.

document.input:
As large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) continue to grow in size, the traditional paradigm of full-parameter fine-tuning has become increasingly impractical due to the massive computational and storage costs involved.
